title: OCC Sandler Call Scoring Schema
description: Scores sales calls based on Sandler Submarine methodology + social layer patterns

compartments.name: Bonding & Rapport
compartments.description: Create trust and emotional safety for candid conversation

detectionCriteria: Uses softening statements: 'Out of curiosity...', 'Help me understand...'
Asks open-ended questions about prospect's world
Demonstrates active listening (references earlier statements)
examples: Out of curiosity, how did you end up responsible for sales enablement?
Help me understand your situation...

detectionCriteria: Uses peer language: 'Let's figure out if this makes sense for both of us'
Avoids subordinate language: 'I'd love to earn your business'
Shows genuine detachment - comfortable with 'no'
examples: This may or may not be a fit, and that's okay
Let's see if this makes sense for both of us

detectionCriteria: Rep talk time < 40% in this phase
Uses silence effectively (pauses 3+ seconds)
Reflects back what prospect says
examples: [3-5 second pause after prospect shares something]
So what I'm hearing is...

detectionCriteria: Mentions product features before pain discussion
Talks about 'what we do' in first 5 minutes
Says 'I'd love to earn your business'
Asks 'When can I follow up with you?'

compartments.name: Up-Front Contract
compartments.description: Set clear expectations: purpose, time, agenda, and that 'no' is okay

detectionCriteria: Explicitly states meeting duration
Asks if time still works
References time in up-front contract
examples: So that we respect your time, I have a few questions...
We said 15 minutes - does that still work?

detectionCriteria: Explicitly gives permission to say no
Uses phrases like 'and that's okay' or 'this may not be a fit'
Eliminates 'maybe' as an option
examples: If we get to the end and it's not a fit, are you comfortable telling me no?
This might not be for you, and that's okay

detectionCriteria: Outlines what will be discussed
Explains the purpose of the call
Gets prospect agreement on agenda
examples: Here's what I'd like to cover... Fair enough?
We said we'd use these few minutes to explore whether there's a reason to keep talking. Does that still work?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'Fair enough?' or similar confirmation
Waits for prospect to verbally agree
Doesn't proceed without agreement
examples: Fair enough?
Does that work for you?
Sound good?

detectionCriteria: Jumps straight into questions without setting expectations
No mention of time, agenda, or permission to say no
Sets time but doesn't make 'no' safe
Doesn't get explicit agreement

compartments.name: Pain (Pain Funnel)
compartments.description: Use Pain Funnel to go from surface symptoms to deep business and personal impact

detectionCriteria: Asks 'Tell me more about that'
Asks 'Can you give me an example?'
Gets prospect talking about surface-level issue
examples: Tell me more about that
Can you give me an example?
What does that look like day-to-day?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'How long has that been an issue?'
Asks 'What does that cost the business?'
Quantifies impact in time or money
examples: How long has that been happening?
What's that costing you?
What does that do to your pipeline?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'How does that affect you personally?'
Asks 'What happens if nothing changes?'
Gets prospect to reveal personal consequences
examples: How does that affect you personally?
What happens if this doesn't get fixed?
How does that make you feel?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'Have you given up trying to fix this?'
Asks 'Why fix it now versus six months from now?'
Tests urgency and commitment
examples: Have you given up on fixing this?
Why now versus waiting?
What's different now that makes this urgent?

requiredBehaviors.behavior: Stays in pain (doesn't jump to solution)
detectionCriteria: No product mentions during pain phase
Asks at least 4-7 pain questions in sequence
Uses silence effectively between questions
examples: [Asks follow-up pain questions instead of pitching]
[Pauses 3-5 seconds after prospect reveals pain]

socialLayerPatterns.description: Pull back before pushing forward - increases psychological desire
detectionCriteria: Uses negative reverse after pain: 'This might not be worth fixing'
Pulls back when prospect shows interest
Creates tension by suggesting it may not be a fit
examples: Honestly, if it's only costing you a few deals a quarter, this might not be worth changing
Maybe sticking with your current approach is actually the best move

socialLayerPatterns.description: Ask 'What then?' or 'And then what?' to flush hidden objections
detectionCriteria: Uses 'What then?' after prospect describes process
Asks 'And then what happens?' to find roadblocks
Goes 3-4 levels deep with 'what then?' questions
examples: So you talk to your VP... what then?
And then what happens?
What has to happen after that?

antiPatterns.pattern: Spills candy in lobby (pitches before pain)
detectionCriteria: Mentions product features before completing pain funnel
Answers 'How does it work?' without reversing
Talks about solution before understanding impact

antiPatterns.pattern: Stays at surface level (doesn't go deep)
detectionCriteria: Asks fewer than 4 pain questions
Doesn't get to personal impact (level 4-5)
Doesn't test commitment (level 6-7)
Rapid-fire questions without pauses
Doesn't acknowledge or reflect pain
Tone feels like cross-examination

compartments.name: Budget
compartments.description: Normalize money talk. Understand ability AND willingness to invest.

detectionCriteria: Explicitly asks about budget
Uses phrases like 'Can we talk about budget?'
Doesn't avoid or delay money discussion
examples: Can we talk a bit about budget so we both know whether it makes sense?
How do you usually decide what's worth investing in?

requiredBehaviors.behavior: Reverses pricing questions (if asked too early)
detectionCriteria: Doesn't give price before pain is established
Reverses with 'It varies - can we talk about what you're solving first?'
Ties budget discussion to pain
examples: It varies quite a bit. Can we talk about what you're trying to solve first?
Fair question - what were you thinking of investing to solve [their pain]?

detectionCriteria: Asks what they were thinking of investing
Explores how they typically budget for this type of solution
Tests if budget is real or just checking price
examples: What were you thinking of investing to solve this?
How does your team typically budget for training reinforcement?

detectionCriteria: Uses negative reverse if budget is too low
Suggests not wasting time if budget isn't there
Walks away cleanly if no budget
examples: If $500/month isn't in the budget, let's not waste your time with a demo
Sounds like budget isn't allocated yet - should we reconnect when it is?

detectionCriteria: Answers 'How much?' before pain funnel complete
Provides pricing without reversing
Spills candy by revealing price too early
Skips budget discussion entirely
Moves to decision without knowing budget
Assumes they'll figure out budget later
Prospect says 'We'll find budget if it makes sense'
Rep doesn't test if budget is real
Moves forward without concrete budget commitment

compartments.name: Decision
compartments.description: Understand WHO decides, HOW they decide, and WHEN

detectionCriteria: Asks 'Besides you, who else is involved?'
Maps out full decision-making committee
Doesn't assume prospect is sole decision maker
examples: Besides you, who else will be involved in deciding?
Who else has to sign off on this?
Talk me through who's in the room when this gets decided

detectionCriteria: Asks 'How do decisions like this usually get made here?'
Maps out steps in decision process
Understands what 'yes' actually looks like procedurally
examples: Walk me through how decisions like this usually get made
What does a 'yes' actually look like procedurally?
What has to happen between now and a signed contract?

detectionCriteria: Asks when decision will be made
Tests if timeline is real or aspirational
Uses negative reverse if timeline seems unrealistic
examples: When are you looking to have this decided?
Is that timeline real or are we just hoping?
What happens if this doesn't get decided by then?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'What then?' after each decision step
Goes 3-4 levels deep to find hidden objections
Discovers roadblocks before they happen
examples: So you present to your VP... what then?
And then what happens?
What could derail this between now and signing?

socialLayerPatterns.description: Accept that their buying process will always win - don't fight it
detectionCriteria: Doesn't try to force rep's process onto prospect
Adapts to prospect's system rather than imposing own
Shows flexibility in how deal progresses
examples: Help me understand how this typically works at your company
What's your normal process for evaluating new vendors?

detectionCriteria: Doesn't ask who else is involved
Moves to fulfillment without mapping decision committee
Gets surprised by 'I need to run this by my boss' later
Prospect says 'I'll take it to the team'
Rep doesn't understand specific steps
No clarity on what happens next
Moves from budget straight to fulfillment
Doesn't understand decision process
No timeline established

compartments.name: Fulfillment
compartments.description: Present solution ONLY after qualifying. Tie everything back to stated pains.

detectionCriteria: Doesn't present until pain, budget, decision are covered
All 5 prior compartments sealed before fulfillment
No premature pitching
examples: [Fulfillment phase starts AFTER compartments 1-5 complete]

requiredBehaviors.behavior: Maps features to their specific pains
detectionCriteria: References pains prospect mentioned earlier
Explicitly connects each feature to a stated pain
Uses their language, not generic benefits
examples: You mentioned reps revert after training - here's how we solve that...
Remember you said it's costing 3-4 deals a quarter? This addresses that by...

detectionCriteria: Doesn't monologue for more than 2-3 minutes
Checks in frequently: 'How does this land?'
Stays in dialogue, not presentation mode
examples: How does this compare to what you were hoping to see?
Does this address what you mentioned earlier about [pain]?
What questions do you have so far?

detectionCriteria: Asks 'How does this land?' or similar
Checks if solution matches their expectations
Uses negative reverse if they seem lukewarm
examples: How does this compare to what you had in mind?
Does this make sense given what you shared?
I'm sensing some hesitation - what's not sitting right?

socialLayerPatterns.description: All compartments must be sealed, but order can flex if prospect pulls you
detectionCriteria: If prospect asks for features early, gives brief answer then reverses back to pain
Doesn't rigidly refuse to answer questions
Eventually covers all compartments even if order flexes
examples: [Prospect asks 'How does it work?' in pain phase]
Quick version: we score every call and coach same-day. But before I go deeper - what specifically are you trying to solve?

detectionCriteria: Jumps to fulfillment before pain/budget/decision
Spills candy in lobby
Leads with features instead of questions

antiPatterns.pattern: Generic pitch (doesn't tie to their pains)
detectionCriteria: Uses generic benefits instead of their specific pains
Doesn't reference what prospect said earlier
Sounds like a canned pitch
Talks for 5+ minutes straight
Doesn't ask 'How does this land?'
Presentation mode instead of conversation

compartments.name: Post-Sell
compartments.description: Prevent buyer's remorse and reinforce decision

detectionCriteria: Summarizes what was agreed upon
Asks if it still feels right
Gives prospect chance to voice concerns
examples: We've agreed on X, Y, Z. Does that still feel right?
Just to recap, we're moving forward with... sound good?

detectionCriteria: Asks who might object internally
Explores how prospect will handle objections
Uses 'What then?' to find roadblocks
examples: Who might push back on this internally?
How do you think they'll react?
What will you say when finance asks why now?

requiredBehaviors.behavior: Establishes success criteria and failure signals
detectionCriteria: Asks what success looks like
Asks what first sign of failure would be
Sets clear expectations for what happens next
examples: What does success look like 90 days from now?
What's the first sign this isn't going as planned?
What would make you regret this decision?

socialLayerPatterns.pattern: Negative reverse at stall points ONLY
socialLayerPatterns.description: Only use negative reverse when deal is stalling, not before
detectionCriteria: Prospect goes dark or stops responding
Timeline slips without explanation
Uses 'Should we take this off the table?' to flush truth
examples: Should we just take this off the table for now?
Honestly, it feels like this may not be the right time
Maybe we should reconnect when this becomes more urgent

detectionCriteria: Closes deal and immediately ends call
Doesn't address potential objections
No discussion of next steps or success criteria
Doesn't ask about internal pushback
Doesn't establish what could go wrong
Overly optimistic without testing

compartmentRequirements.description: All 7 compartments must be 'sealed' (covered), but order can flex if prospect pulls rep in different direction. However, fulfillment should always come AFTER pain, budget, and decision.

detectionCriteria: Voice doesn't spike with excitement
Doesn't sound desperate or needy
Uses negative reverse genuinely
Questions sound exploratory
Doesn't rapid-fire questions
Uses pauses and silence
Confident without arrogance
Doesn't use submissive language
Comfortable challenging prospect

socialLayerBonus.description: Bonus points for advanced Sandler techniques like Pendulum, 'What then?', timing negative reverse correctly, and adapting to prospect's system

highPriority.trigger: Spills candy in lobby (pitches before pain)
highPriority.coachingMessage: You jumped to features before understanding their pain. Go back to Pain Funnel - get to level 6-7 before presenting solution.
highPriority.referenceMaterial: Sandler Rule: Don't Spill Your Candy in the Lobby

highPriority.coachingMessage: You didn't set an up-front contract. Before diving in, set time, agenda, and make 'no' safe. Ask: 'Fair enough?'

highPriority.trigger: Stays at surface pain (doesn't go deep)
highPriority.coachingMessage: You only scratched the surface. Use the Pain Funnel to go deeper: How long? What's it cost? How does it affect YOU personally? Why now vs 6 months?
highPriority.referenceMaterial: Pain Funnel: 7 levels from surface to commitment

highPriority.coachingMessage: You didn't map the decision process. Ask: 'Besides you, who else is involved?' Then use 'What then?' to flush roadblocks.

mediumPriority.coachingMessage: When they ask price before pain, reverse with: 'It varies - can we talk about what you're solving first?'
You talked for too long without checking alignment. Ask: 'How does this land?' every 2-3 minutes.
mediumPriority.referenceMaterial: Submarine Compartment 6: Fulfillment - keep it conversational

mediumPriority.coachingMessage: After they agree, recap what was decided and ask: 'Who might push back internally? How will you handle that?'
mediumPriority.referenceMaterial: Submarine Compartment 7: Post-Sell - prevent buyer's remorse

opportunityForExcellence.coachingMessage: Great job on pain. Now try pulling back: 'If this is only costing a few deals, maybe it's not worth changing.' Watch them lean in.

opportunityForExcellence.trigger: Could use 'What then?' to flush roadblocks
opportunityForExcellence.coachingMessage: Nice work on decision process. Now go deeper with 'What then?' after each step to find hidden roadblocks.
Good rapport, but avoid subordinate language like 'I'd love to earn your business.' Instead: 'Let's see if this makes sense for both of us.'
